metadata.title: policy - Password and Credential Management Policy

components.title: Password and Credential Management Policy
components.description: This policy sets the organisation's requirements for password/passphrase construction, storage, and management, and for use of the organisation's password manager and Privileged Access Management vault for storing and generating credentials. It complements the Access Control Policy by focusing specifically on how credentials themselves are constructed and protected, rather than who is granted access.
components.purpose: Ensure passwords and other credentials used across the organisation's systems are constructed, stored, and managed in a way that resists guessing, cracking, and credential-stuffing attacks.

control-implementations.description: Control implementations for the Password and Credential Management Policy.

implemented-requirements.description: Credentials stored on organisational systems are protected by a password manager, a hardware security module, or by salting, hashing, and stretching them before storage — never stored in plaintext or reversibly-encrypted form.
User account passwords do not use reversible encryption for storage; only salted and stretched cryptographic hashes are used.

Passwords appearing in lists of commonly used or previously compromised passwords are not permitted, enforced via a compromised-password check at password set/change time where the identity platform supports it.
Where a password is used, the maximum length limit imposed by the system is not less than 64 characters, allowing the use of long passphrases.
Where a password is used, all ASCII printable characters are supported, so users are not artificially restricted from using strong passphrase constructions.
Credentials for the built-in Administrator account in each Windows domain are long, unique, unpredictable, and managed via Microsoft LAPS (or Entra LAPS) rather than a shared, memorised password.

Credentials for break glass accounts, local administrator accounts, and service accounts are long, unique, unpredictable, and managed via the organisation's Privileged Access Management vault rather than manually set and remembered.

Where a system genuinely cannot support multi-factor authentication, single-factor password authentication is permitted only as a documented exception, with a correspondingly longer minimum password length required.
